content.text: Weather is the state of the Earth's atmosphere at a particular time and place. The atmosphere is the layer of gases (air) that surrounds our planet, much like a layered cake.

content.title: The Troposphere
content.text: The layer closest to the Earth's surface is the troposphere. This is where all land-based plants and animals live, where we breathe, and where almost all weather phenomena take place. It extends to a height of 6 to 18 kilometres from the ground.
The troposphere's thickness varies—it is thinner at the cold poles (where air contracts) and thicker in the tropical zones (where warmer air expands).
We use various terms like hot, rainy, or windy to describe the weather. These terms reflect the five measurable elements of weather.

content.title: Elements of Weather
items: Temperature: How hot or cold the air is.
Precipitation: Any form of water, such as rain, snow, sleet, or hail, falling from the sky.
Atmospheric Pressure: The weight of the air above us felt on the Earth's surface.
Wind: The movement of air, including its speed and direction.
Humidity: The amount of water vapour (water in gaseous form) in the air.

content.text: Temperature is measured using thermometers. While clinical and laboratory thermometers exist, meteorologists use specialized instruments to record ambient temperature or daily extremes.

content.title: Temperature Scales
content.text: The most common scales are Celsius (°C) and Fahrenheit (°F). For example, a temperature of 15°C is equivalent to 59°F.

content.title: Calculating Temperature Statistics
items: Range of Temperature: The maximum temperature minus the minimum temperature in a 24-hour period.
Mean Daily Temperature: The maximum plus the minimum temperature of the day, divided by two.

content.text: Rainfall is measured using a 'Rain Gauge'. It consists of a collecting funnel and a measuring cylinder with a scale attached.

content.title: Measuring Units
content.text: The amount is measured in millimetres (mm). For instance, if the depth of water collected is 5 mm, we say the area received 5 mm of rainfall.

content.title: Extreme Precipitation
content.text: Sleet refers to frozen or partly frozen rain; Hail refers to small, hard balls of ice that fall from the sky.
Atmospheric pressure is the weight exerted by the air above and around us. It is highest at sea level and decreases as we go higher into the mountains.

content.title: Effects of Altitude
content.text: At high altitudes (like Ladakh), the air gets thinner and pressure is lower. Since there is less oxygen available, people often feel breathless, dizzy, or tired until they 'acclimatise' (adjust to the new conditions).

Pressure is measured using a Barometer in units called millibars (mb). Normal pressure at the sea coast is around 1013 mb. A drop below 1000 mb is called a 'depression', which can lead to storms or cyclones.
Wind is the movement of air from areas of high pressure to areas of low pressure. Wind direction and speed are crucial for pilots, sailors, and farmers.

rows: Gives pilots indicative direction during take-off/landing

content.text: Humidity refers to the amount of water vapour in the air. It is usually measured as 'Relative Humidity' (%) using a 'Hygrometer'.

content.title: The Evaporation Rule
content.text: Evaporation causes a cooling effect. If the humidity is high (on a rainy day), water evaporates slowly, making us sweat more and clothes dry slower. In dry weather (humidity 20–40%), evaporation is faster.
Before modern technology, humans traditionaly forecasted weather by observing nature: ants shifting eggs to higher ground, frogs croaking loudly, or pine cones closing in high humidity.

content.title: Meteorology
content.text: Meteorology is the systematic study of weather and its evolution. This study forms the scientific basis for all modern weather predictions.
A 'Weather Station' brings together all instruments to map and track weather. Modern versions are often Automated Weather Stations (AWS).

content.title: The India Meteorological Department (IMD)
content.text: Established in 1875, the IMD monitors weather across India. Its motto translates to: 'From the sun arises rain'.
Accurate predictions save lives by alerting people to disasters like cyclones, heat waves, or floods. Fishermen depend on these alerts before venturing into the sea.

glossary.definition: The layer of the atmosphere closest to the Earth's surface where almost all weather phenomena take place.
Any form of water—rain, snow, sleet, or hail—that falls from the sky.
The pressure exerted by the weight of the air above and around us.
An instrument used to measure atmospheric pressure, usually in millibars (mb).
To adjust to a new climate or new conditions, such as the low oxygen at high altitudes.
An instrument with rotating cups used to measure wind speed.
The amount of water vapour present in the air.
An instrument used to measure the relative humidity of the air.
The systematic study of weather and its evolution.
To predict or find out in advance what the weather will be like.
Automated Weather Station; a self-operating system that uses sensors to measure and record weather data.

summary_points: Weather is the temporary state of the Earth's atmosphere at a given time and place, primarily occurring in the troposphere layer.
The five elements that define weather are temperature, precipitation, atmospheric pressure, wind, and humidity.
Temperature is measured in Celsius or Fahrenheit, and its range and daily mean are important statistical records.
Precipitation includes rain, snow, sleet, and hail, and is precisely measured in millimetres using a rain gauge.
Atmospheric pressure decreases with altitude, requiring people to acclimatise to thinner air at high elevations.
A rapid drop in atmospheric pressure (below 1000 mb) is called a depression and often indicates a coming storm or cyclone.
Wind speed is measured using an anemometer, while its direction is tracked by a wind vane or wind sock.
Humidity is measured as relative humidity by a hygrometer; high humidity slows down evaporation and cooling.
Humans historically predicted weather by observing animal behaviors, but modern meteorology relies on systematic data and AWS.
Accurate weather forecasting by agencies like the IMD is essential for disaster preparedness and public safety.
